general:
About

This website accompanies a research project at TU Berlin and UdK Berlin about children's scribbles. The project asks what these marks are, if you set aside the question of what they represent or what they reveal about the child who made them.

Since the late nineteenth century, researchers and clinicians have tended to interpret children's drawings either as stages on the way to figurative representation, or as diagnostic material. The artist Mike Kelley demonstrated how readily this interpretive tendency becomes projection in his 1995 work We Communicate Only Through Our Shared Dismissal of the Pre-linguistic, in which he wrote invented psychiatric captions for children's drawings, showing how easily adults impose pathology and hierarchy onto marks that simply have their own logic [1].

The American researcher Rhoda Kellogg spent decades collecting over a million children's drawings from around the world and found that the same basic forms keep appearing everywhere, in the same developmental order, regardless of culture [2]. The Swiss researchers Dieter Maurer and Claudia Riboni, working at the Zürcher Hochschule der Künste, extended this research over several decades and proposed that formal, non-representational graphic organisation always precedes any attempt at depiction [3]. The child does not begin by trying to draw things. The child begins with form.

The central hypothesis of this research is that those early, non-referential forms may share structural properties with what cognitive scientists call image schemata: pre-linguistic patterns of embodied experience, such as containment, path, balance, and force, that organise perception and thought before and below the level of language [4][5]. If this is the case, children's scribbles might offer a way to study, visualise, and enact these cognitive structures in material and temporal form. The website explores this hypothesis through four tools: a visual taxonomy, an annotation system, a drawing studio, and an association map.
taxonomy:
Kellogg's original classification covers many more categories than the ones presented here. Her full taxonomy includes figurative groups such as Humans, Animals, Buildings, Vegetation, and Transportation. This research focuses exclusively on the non-referential portion of the taxonomy, from Basic Scribbles through Placement Patterns, Emergent Diagram Shapes, Diagrams, Combines, Aggregates, Mandalas, Suns, and Radials.

This choice is not arbitrary. It follows the central finding of Maurer and Riboni's research: that abstract, purely formal graphic phenomena appear and develop autonomously, before any figurative or analogical relation to objects emerges [3]. The categories selected here are those in which marks do not yet point to anything outside themselves. They are not failed attempts at depiction. They are, as Maurer and Riboni argue, a primary and independent domain of graphic organisation that precedes representation and cannot be reduced to it. For this reason, the figurative categories of Kellogg's taxonomy fall outside the scope of this project.

There is a further reason this reconstruction exists. Kellogg's original taxonomy is accessible only through her printed books, which are out of print and expensive, or through scans available on the Internet Archive. The Internet Archive is, at the time of writing, under sustained legal and political pressure from large commercial interests seeking to restrict free access to digitised knowledge, pressure that reflects a broader effort by corporations and governments to enclose, commodify, and control the cultural commons. The scans that do exist are also of limited quality. To our knowledge, no complete, clean, and openly accessible visual reference for the non-referential portion of Kellogg's taxonomy currently exists.

Beyond accessibility, there is a methodological reason to redo this work from scratch. The illustrations in Kellogg's books are static diagrams. The drawings in this taxonomy are not. Each one was produced on a digital tablet and saved as a full spatiotemporal recording: every point drawn, in sequence, with its timestamp and pressure value, in addition to a static image and an animated version. This means each illustration is also a record of how it was made, preserving the gesture, the timing, and the movement that produced it. This is relevant to understanding the procedural logic that Maurer and Riboni identify as fundamental to early graphic development [3], and it is potentially useful as training material for computational models intended to recognise and classify these forms.
annotator:
The Kellogg archive available at early-pictures.ch [6] is an invaluable research resource: thousands of scanned children's drawings, carefully organised and made freely accessible by the research group of Dieter Maurer and Claudia Riboni. The archive was designed as an empirical and pedagogical tool for human researchers, and it works well for that purpose.

For the purposes of building a computational dataset, however, it presents a structural limitation that has nothing to do with the quality of the archive itself. Each drawing is assigned a single primary category. Most drawings, however, contain elements from several levels of the taxonomy simultaneously. A drawing classified as a Sun will also contain a closed circle, placement pattern elements, and possibly radial lines and other sub-structures. A single label does not capture this layered composition.

The annotator is a tool developed to address this. It allows drawings from the archive to be assigned multiple simultaneous labels, distinguishing between the spatial organisation of the drawing on the page, its primary graphic configuration, and any additional co-present elements. Every change is logged with a full audit trail. Visitors to this website can browse the annotations in read-only mode.
is_studio:
Image schemata are a concept introduced simultaneously and independently by Mark Johnson in The Body in the Mind (1987) [4] and George Lakoff in Women, Fire, and Dangerous Things (1987) [5]. The theory proposes that a significant part of human conceptual structure, including abstract reasoning, is grounded in recurring patterns of sensorimotor experience. These patterns, which Johnson and Lakoff call image schemata, emerge from the body's repeated interactions with the physical world: the experience of being inside or outside something gives rise to the CONTAINER schema; the experience of moving from one place to another gives rise to the PATH schema; the experience of maintaining equilibrium gives rise to the BALANCE schema; the experience of exerting or resisting force gives rise to a family of force schemata including COMPULSION, BLOCKAGE, COUNTERFORCE, and ENABLEMENT. The theory has remained central to cognitive linguistics and embodied cognition research, and has been developed and refined by subsequent researchers including Hampe and Grady [7], Mandler [7], and Zlatev [7], among others.

One recurring difficulty in this field is the visual representation of image schemata. Because they are dynamic structures that unfold in time and involve change, force, and spatial relation, static diagrams can only partially capture them. The diagrams used across the cognitive linguistics literature have no shared formal language and are not mutually comparable. The Diagrammatic Image Schema Language (DISL), developed by Hedblom, Neuhaus, and Mossakowski (2025) [8], represents the most systematic attempt so far to address this: a standardised notation in which each schema is decomposed into conceptual primitives and described as a sequence of spatial scenes. DISL is itself a static notation in the sense that it produces a strip of discrete frames, but it is formally defined and internally consistent in a way that previous informal diagrams were not, and it can in principle be serialised and processed computationally. A related research direction by Olivier and Bouraoui (2025) [9] argues that any adequate formalism for image schemata must be qualitative, typed, temporal, and capable of non-monotonic reasoning, since image schemata involve default behaviours, force dynamics, and change over time.

The IS Studio is a speculative tool based on the hypothesis at the centre of this research: that children's scribbles, when recorded as full spatiotemporal sequences, might offer a way to enact and explore image schemata in material form. The association map proposes structural correspondences between Kellogg graphic categories and image schemata. A spiral, for example, shares structural features with PATH, CYCLE, and COMPULSION. A closed circle shares structural features with CONTAINER. A radial pattern shares structural features with CENTER-PERIPHERY and BALANCE. Whether these correspondences are meaningful in a deeper cognitive sense is an open question this research does not claim to resolve. The studio offers a way to explore them in practice.
map:
The association map connects the visual taxonomy reconstructed in this project, covering the non-referential portion of Kellogg's classification, to an inventory of image schemata. It is presented as an interactive network graph. Each node represents either a Kellogg graphic category or an image schema. Edges connect them according to proposed structural correspondences, weighted to distinguish primary from secondary associations.

The map is speculative. It is not derived from empirical data about what children think or intend when they draw, which is not directly accessible. It is derived from the structural properties of the graphic forms themselves, read against the definitions of image schemata in the cognitive linguistics literature. A closed circle produces an inside and an outside: this corresponds structurally to the CONTAINER schema. A cross connects two axes at a point: this corresponds structurally to LINK and CONTACT. The map proposes that these correspondences are consistent enough to be worth making explicit, and uses them as the basis for the drawing instructions in the IS Studio. It is a working document and will be revised as the annotation dataset grows and as IS Studio sessions accumulate.
